guardrail_evaluations.explanation: The 15% discount is well below the 50% absolute ceiling. No structural rebuild required on discount alone.
At 15%, the discount sits comfortably below the 35% CFO approval threshold. No executive sign-off required for the base discount.
The 15% discount is within the standard enterprise envelope of up to 25%. Document the multi-year volume and strategic logo rationale in the deal record.

The ramp schedule covers exactly 6 months (months 1–6 at 50% of full rate), which meets but does not exceed the soft ceiling. However, the ramp structure effectively increases the economic discount in Year 1 — see margin note.

Estimated gross margin of ~29.4% clears the 25% absolute floor, but only modestly. Note this does not account for the ramp subsidy, $200K in data-provider credits, or billing-in-arrears carrying costs, all of which compress effective margin further.

Estimated margin of ~29.4% falls below the 40% yellow-flag threshold. The non-standard concessions (ramp, $200K credits, arrears billing) will push the effective margin even lower — rerun the economics after pricing in all concessions.

alternative_structures.label: Ramp-to-Full with Escalator and Reduced Credits
alternative_structures.description: Maintain the 6-month ramp at 50% but reduce the data-provider credits from $200K to $100K, eliminate the MFN clause, and replace the termination-for-convenience right with a mutual termination-for-cause provision. Offer a modest additional list discount (17%) in exchange for removing the MFN and T4C clause.
alternative_structures.rationale: Trading a slightly deeper headline discount for elimination of the MFN and T4C clause substantially reduces pricing liability across the portfolio and removes churn optionality at month 13. The $100K credit reduction directly recovers margin. The net ACV concession is small but the structural risk reduction is significant.

alternative_structures.label: 36-Month Commit with Annual Escalator and No MFN
alternative_structures.description: Keep the 36-month term but structure with a 3% annual escalator (Year 1 at $500K ACV, Year 2 at $515K, Year 3 at $530.5K). Remove the MFN clause entirely, convert billing to annual in advance (not arrears), and cap data-provider credits at $100K allocated in Year 1 only. Retain the 6-month ramp.
alternative_structures.rationale: The escalator converts a flat $1.5M TCV deal into ~$1.545M TCV, improving total contract value by ~$45K and recovering some ramp subsidy cost in Years 2–3. Annual billing in advance eliminates the working capital drag of arrears. Removing the MFN prevents downstream pricing exposure to other AI lab customers, which is the highest-risk structural element of the current deal.

alternative_structures.label: Usage-Commit Floor with Overage Upside and No T4C
alternative_structures.description: Restructure the fixed ACV into a hybrid usage-commit model: $420K annual usage floor (reflecting the ramp-period economics) with overage billed at a published rate card. Eliminate both the MFN clause and the termination-for-convenience right. Convert the $200K credits into a performance milestone: $100K unlocked at contract signing, $100K unlocked upon Anthropic reaching 80% of the usage floor in month 9. Billing moves to quarterly in advance.
alternative_structures.rationale: The lower floor reduces Anthropic's downside risk (addressing their build-vs-buy concern) while overage upside creates revenue leverage if their AI tooling use cases scale as projected. Eliminating T4C removes the churn cliff at month 13. The lower floor ACV is a concession, but the overage structure and milestone-gated credits tie Clay's revenue growth to Anthropic's actual product adoption — reducing risk of underutilization and early exit. Note: margin at the floor price falls below the 40% warn threshold and approaches the 25% floor; this structure is only viable if overage materially lifts effective ACV above $480K within 18 months.

reasoning_summary: The headline 15% discount is clean — it passes all discount guardrails comfortably and requires no CFO sign-off on the base price. However, the effective economics are materially worse than the headline suggests: the 6-month 50% ramp reduces Year 1 realized revenue by ~$125K, the $200K in pre-paid data-provider credits convert TCV into non-cash concessions, and annual billing in arrears introduces working capital cost that further compresses margin. Under the standard 40%-at-list margin assumption (flagged as a working assumption — actual COGS should be confirmed with Finance), the estimated gross margin is ~29.4%, which triggers the 40% warn guardrail. The MFN clause and 60-day termination-for-convenience right starting month 13 are the highest structural risks in the deal and should be the primary negotiation levers before re-quoting. No precedent deals were available to benchmark against.
